body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 170' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2' fill='#bfdbfe'><rect x='20' y='20' width='40' height='40'/><rect x='60' y='20' width='40' height='40'/><rect x='100' y='20' width='40' height='40'/><rect x='140' y='20' width='40' height='40'/><rect x='20' y='60' width='40' height='40'/><rect x='60' y='60' width='40' height='40'/><rect x='100' y='60' width='40' height='40'/><rect x='140' y='60' width='40' height='40'/><rect x='20' y='100' width='40' height='40'/><rect x='60' y='100' width='40' height='40'/><rect x='100' y='100' width='40' height='40'/><rect x='140' y='100' width='40' height='40'/></g><text x='100' y='160' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>3 rows x 4 columns = 12 square units</text></svg>

body.text: Two Ways to Find Area

items: Count every unit square inside the rectangle.
Multiply the number of rows by the number of columns.

body.text: In the picture there are 3 rows and 4 columns. We can count 12 squares, or we can multiply 3 x 4 = 12. Both ways give 12 square units.

body.title: Shortcut
body.text: Multiplying rows times columns is faster than counting every square, and you get the same answer!

prompt: A rectangle has 5 rows and 2 columns of unit squares. What is its area?

options.rationale: Rows times columns is 5 x 2 = 10 square units.

explanation: Multiply rows by columns: 5 x 2 = 10. The area is 10 square units.

prompt: A rectangle is tiled with 4 rows and 4 columns of unit squares. What is the area?

options.rationale: 4 rows times 4 columns is 4 x 4 = 16 square units.

explanation: Multiply rows by columns: 4 x 4 = 16. The area is 16 square units.

prompt: A rectangle has 6 unit squares in each of its 3 rows. What is the total area?

options.rationale: 3 rows of 6 squares is 3 x 6 = 18 square units.

explanation: There are 3 rows with 6 squares each, so 3 x 6 = 18 square units.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Area of Composite Figures Made of Two Rectangles
lessons.intro: Sometimes a shape is made of two rectangles joined together. In this lesson you will break the shape into two non-overlapping rectangles, find each area, and add them. This helps you find the area of an L-shaped figure.

vocabulary.meaning: A shape made by joining two or more simple shapes.
To break a shape into smaller parts.
The amount of flat space inside a shape.
Parts that do not cover each other or share inside space.
The whole area found by adding the areas of the parts.

body.text: Breaking a Shape into Two Rectangles
An L-shaped figure can be cut into two rectangles that do not overlap. Find the area of each rectangle, then add them together.

body.alt: An L-shaped figure split by a dashed line into two rectangles, one 4 by 2 and one 2 by 3.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 200' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='20,20 100,20 100,80 60,80 60,160 20,160' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='20' y1='80' x2='100' y2='80' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6 4'/><text x='60' y='55' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>4 x 2 = 8</text><text x='40' y='125' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>2 x 3 = 6</text><text x='120' y='185' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>8 + 6 = 14 square units</text></svg>

body.text: Steps to Find Total Area

items: Draw a line to split the figure into two rectangles.
Find the area of the first rectangle (rows x columns).
Find the area of the second rectangle.
Add the two areas to get the total area.

body.html:
Top rectangle: 4 x 2 = 8 square units.
Bottom rectangle: 2 x 3 = 6 square units.
Total area: 8 + 6 = 14 square units.

body.title: Watch Out
body.text: Make sure your two rectangles do not overlap, or you will count some squares twice.

prompt: A figure splits into a rectangle with area 9 square units and a rectangle with area 5 square units. What is the total area?

options.rationale: Add the two areas: 9 + 5 = 14 square units.

explanation: Add the area of each rectangle: 9 + 5 = 14 square units.

prompt: An L-shape is made of a 3 x 2 rectangle and a 2 x 2 rectangle. What is the total area?

options.rationale: 3 x 2 = 6 and 2 x 2 = 4, then 6 + 4 = 10 square units.

explanation: First rectangle: 3 x 2 = 6. Second rectangle: 2 x 2 = 4. Total: 6 + 4 = 10 square units.

prompt: A shape is split into a 5 x 1 rectangle and a 5 x 2 rectangle. What is the total area?

options.rationale: 5 x 1 = 5 and 5 x 2 = 10, then 5 + 10 = 15 square units.

explanation: First rectangle: 5 x 1 = 5. Second rectangle: 5 x 2 = 10. Total: 5 + 10 = 15 square units.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Naming Equal Parts of a Whole Square as Unit Fractions of Area
lessons.intro: When you split one whole square into equal parts, each part is a unit fraction of the whole area. In this lesson you will learn to name each equal piece, like one half or one fourth. The parts must be the same size.

vocabulary.meaning: The complete shape, like one full square.
Parts that are the same size.
A fraction that shows one equal part, like 1/2 or 1/4.
The bottom number that tells how many equal parts the whole is split into.
The amount of flat space inside a shape.

body.text: Splitting One Square into Equal Parts
Start with one whole square. If you cut it into equal parts, each part has the same area. We name each part with a unit fraction.

body.alt: A square split into 4 equal parts, with one part shaded and labeled one fourth.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='20' width='100' height='100' fill='none' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='30' y='20' width='50' height='50' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='80' y='20' width='50' height='50' fill='none' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='30' y='70' width='50' height='50' fill='none' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='80' y='70' width='50' height='50' fill='none' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2'/><text x='150' y='75' font-size='14' fill='#111'>1/4</text><text x='80' y='140' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>4 equal parts</text></svg>

body.text: Naming Each Part
If a square is split into 4 equal parts, each part is 1/4 of the whole. The bottom number, 4, tells how many equal parts there are.

items: 2 equal parts: each part is 1/2.
3 equal parts: each part is 1/3.
4 equal parts: each part is 1/4.
8 equal parts: each part is 1/8.

body.title: Important
body.text: The parts must be EQUAL in size. If the pieces are different sizes, you cannot name them with a simple unit fraction.

prompt: A square is split into 2 equal parts. What is the area of each part?

options.rationale: Two equal parts means each part is one half, or 1/2.

explanation: The square has 2 equal parts, so each part is 1/2 of the whole.

prompt: A whole square is split into 3 equal parts. What unit fraction names each part?

options.rationale: Three equal parts means each part is 1/3 of the whole.

explanation: With 3 equal parts, each part is one third, written 1/3.

prompt: Which square shows equal parts that can be named with a unit fraction?

options.text: A square split into pieces that are all different sizes
A square split into 4 same-size pieces
options.rationale: Equal parts must be the same size, so 4 same-size pieces can be named 1/4 each.

options.text: A square with one big piece and one tiny piece

answer: B. A square split into 4 same-size pieces
explanation: Unit fractions name equal parts. The parts must all be the same size, like 4 same-size pieces.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Fractions as Distances from Zero
lessons.intro: A fraction can show how far a point is from 0 on a number line. In this lesson you will measure the distance from zero to name fractions.

vocabulary.meaning: How far one point is from another.
A line with numbers placed in order.
The starting point where you begin measuring.
The top number that counts the jumps from 0.
The bottom number that tells the size of each jump.
The equal space between two ticks on the line.

body.text: Start at Zero
On a number line, a fraction shows the distance from 0 to a point. Each equal space between ticks is one unit fraction. Count the spaces from 0 to find the fraction.

body.alt: A number line from 0 to 1 split into eighths with a dot at three eighths, three spaces from zero.

body.text: Each space is 1/8. The dot is 3 spaces from 0, so its distance from zero is 3/8.
Measuring the Distance

items: Find how many equal spaces fit from 0 to 1. That is the bottom number.
Count the spaces from 0 to the point. That is the top number.
The fraction is the distance from 0.

body.html: On this line each space is one third. A dot 2 spaces from 0 is at a distance of 2/3.

body.alt: A number line from 0 to 1 split into thirds with a dot at two thirds, two spaces from zero.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 340 70' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='320' y2='40' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='20' y1='30' x2='20' y2='50' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='120' y1='32' x2='120' y2='48' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='32' x2='220' y2='48' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='320' y1='30' x2='320' y2='50' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='220' cy='40' r='7' fill='#cc0000'/><text x='15' y='65' font-size='12'>0</text><text x='316' y='65' font-size='12'>1</text></svg>

body.title: Tip
body.text: Always measure the distance starting from 0, counting each equal space.

prompt: On a number line split into fourths, a dot is 1 space from 0. What is its distance from zero?

options.rationale: Each space is 1/4 and the dot is 1 space from 0, so the distance from zero is 1/4.

explanation: One space of size 1/4 from 0 gives a distance of 1/4.

prompt: On a number line split into sixths, a dot is 5 spaces from 0. What is its distance from zero?

options.rationale: Each space is 1/6 and the dot is 5 spaces from 0, so the distance is 5/6.

explanation: Five spaces of 1/6 each measured from 0 is a distance of 5/6.

prompt: A dot sits right on 0 on the number line. What is its distance from zero?

options.rationale: A point on 0 is no spaces away from 0, so its distance from zero is 0.

explanation: The dot has not moved from 0, so the distance from zero is 0.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Perimeter of Polygons and Finding a Missing Side Length
lessons.intro: Perimeter is the distance all the way around the outside of a shape. In this lesson you will add up the side lengths to find perimeter. You will also find a missing side when you know the perimeter and the other sides.

vocabulary.meaning: The total distance around the outside of a shape.
A closed flat shape made of straight sides.
How long one side of a shape is.
The answer you get when you add numbers together.
A side length you do not know yet and need to find.

body.text: What Is Perimeter?
Perimeter is the distance around the outside of a shape. To find it, add up the lengths of all the sides.

body.alt: A rectangle with side lengths labeled 6, 4, 6, and 4, with perimeter 20.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='40' y='30' width='120' height='80' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2'/><text x='100' y='22' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>6</text><text x='100' y='128' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>6</text><text x='28' y='75' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>4</text><text x='172' y='75' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>4</text><text x='120' y='150' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>6 + 4 + 6 + 4 = 20</text></svg>

body.text: Adding the Sides
The rectangle above has sides 6, 4, 6, and 4. Add them: 6 + 4 + 6 + 4 = 20. The perimeter is 20 units.
Finding a Missing Side
If you know the perimeter and all but one side, you can find the missing side. Add the sides you know, then subtract from the perimeter.

body.html: A triangle has a perimeter of 15. Two sides are 5 and 6. Add them: 5 + 6 = 11. Then subtract: 15 - 11 = 4. The missing side is 4.

body.title: Two Steps
body.text: To find a missing side: 1) Add the known sides. 2) Subtract that sum from the perimeter.

prompt: A square has 4 sides that are each 5 units long. What is its perimeter?

options.rationale: Add all 4 sides: 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 20 units.

explanation: A square has 4 equal sides. Add them: 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 20 units.

prompt: A triangle has a perimeter of 12 units. Two of its sides are 3 units and 4 units. What is the missing side?

options.rationale: Add the known sides: 3 + 4 = 7. Then 12 - 7 = 5 units.

explanation: Add the known sides: 3 + 4 = 7. Subtract from the perimeter: 12 - 7 = 5 units.

prompt: A rectangle has sides 8, 2, 8, and 2 units. What is its perimeter?

options.rationale: Add all the sides: 8 + 2 + 8 + 2 = 20 units.

explanation: Add every side: 8 + 2 + 8 + 2 = 20 units.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Adding and Subtracting Time Intervals in Minutes
lessons.intro: Time problems ask how long something takes or what time it will be. In this lesson you will add and subtract minutes using a clock and a number line. This helps you find start times, end times, and how much time has passed.

vocabulary.meaning: The amount of time between a start and an end, measured in minutes.
How much time has passed from the start to the end.
The time when something begins.
The time when something finishes.
A unit of time. There are 60 minutes in one hour.

body.text: Using a Number Line for Time
A time number line helps you add or subtract minutes. Start at one time and jump forward to add minutes.

body.alt: A number line of time showing a jump of 20 minutes from 3:10 to 3:30.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 110' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='70' x2='290' y2='70' stroke='#111' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='60' x2='60' y2='80' stroke='#111' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='260' y1='60' x2='260' y2='80' stroke='#111' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M60 55 Q160 15 260 55' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='160' y='30' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#dc2626'>+20 minutes</text><text x='60' y='98' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>3:10</text><text x='260' y='98' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>3:30</text></svg>

body.text: Adding Minutes
If recess starts at 3:10 and lasts 20 minutes, count forward: 3:10 plus 20 minutes is 3:30. Recess ends at 3:30.
Subtracting Minutes
If a movie ends at 4:00 and it was 30 minutes long, count back: 4:00 minus 30 minutes is 3:30. The movie started at 3:30.

body.html:
Add minutes to find an end time.
Subtract minutes to find a start time.

body.title: Remember
body.text: There are 60 minutes in one hour. When you pass 60 minutes, you move to the next hour.

prompt: A class starts at 2:15 and lasts 30 minutes. What time does it end?
explanation: Add the minutes: 2:15 plus 30 minutes is 2:45.

prompt: A game ended at 5:00. It lasted 20 minutes. What time did it start?

options.rationale: Subtract 20 minutes from 5:00: 5:00 - 20 = 4:40.

explanation: Count back 20 minutes from 5:00 to get 4:40.

prompt: Maria read from 6:00 to 6:25. How many minutes did she read?

options.rationale: From 6:00 to 6:25 is 25 minutes of elapsed time.

explanation: From 6:00 to 6:25 is 25 minutes. That is the elapsed time.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Choosing Between Liquid Volume and Weight with the Right Units
lessons.intro: Some things we measure by how much space they fill, and some by how heavy they are. In this lesson you will decide when to measure liquid volume and when to measure weight. You will also pick the best unit for each job.

vocabulary.meaning: How much liquid a container holds, also called capacity.
How heavy an object is.
A metric unit for measuring liquid volume.
A metric unit for measuring the weight of light objects.
A metric unit for measuring the weight of heavy objects.
A metric unit for measuring small amounts of liquid.

body.text: Volume or Weight?
Measure liquid volume when you want to know how much a container holds. Measure weight when you want to know how heavy something is.

body.alt: A measuring cup of liquid next to a scale weighing an apple.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 150' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M30 40 L90 40 L85 120 L35 120 Z' fill='none' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M37 80 L88 80 L85 120 L35 120 Z' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='1'/><text x='60' y='140' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>liquid volume</text><line x1='200' y1='40' x2='200' y2='110' stroke='#111' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='40' x2='240' y2='40' stroke='#111' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='180' cy='55' r='12' fill='#86efac' stroke='#15803d' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='220' cy='55' r='12' fill='#fca5a5' stroke='#b91c1c' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='180' y='100' width='40' height='20' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#111' stroke-width='1'/><text x='210' y='140' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle' fill='#111'>weight</text></svg>

body.text: Picking the Right Unit

items: Liquid volume: use milliliters for small amounts and liters for large amounts.
Weight: use grams for light objects and kilograms for heavy objects.

body.html:
What to MeasureGood Units
Juice in a bottleliters or milliliters
A bag of appleskilograms or grams

body.title: Ask Yourself
body.text: Does the question ask how much it holds, or how heavy it is? Holds means volume. Heavy means weight.

prompt: You want to know how much milk fits in a jug. What should you measure?

options.rationale: How much a jug holds is liquid volume, measured in liters or milliliters.

explanation: How much a container holds is liquid volume. Milk in a jug is measured by volume.

prompt: Which unit is best for measuring the weight of a heavy backpack?

options.rationale: Kilograms measure weight, and they are best for heavy objects like a backpack.

explanation: Weight of a heavy object is best measured in kilograms.

prompt: Which unit would you use for a small amount of medicine in a spoon?

options.rationale: Milliliters measure small amounts of liquid, like medicine in a spoon.

explanation: A small amount of liquid is measured in milliliters.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Measuring Liquid Volume and Weight with the Right Tools and Units
lessons.intro: To measure correctly, you need the right tool and the right unit. In this lesson you will use a measuring cup to find liquid volume and a scale to find weight. You will read the marks to find the amount.

vocabulary.meaning: The amount of liquid a container can hold.
A tool with marks used to measure liquid volume.
A tool used to measure the weight of an object.
A metric unit for measuring liquid volume.
A metric unit for measuring weight.
A smart guess about how much or how heavy something is.

body.text: Reading a Measuring Cup
A measuring cup has marks for liquid volume. Find the top of the liquid and read the mark next to it.

body.alt: A measuring cup with marks at 100, 200, 300, and 400 milliliters, filled to the 300 mark.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 180' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M50 20 L150 20 L140 160 L60 160 Z' fill='none' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M64 70 L136 70 L140 160 L60 160 Z' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#1d4ed8' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='60' y1='160' x2='75' y2='160' stroke='#111' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='62' y1='125' x2='77' y2='125' stroke='#111' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='64' y1='95' x2='79' y2='95' stroke='#111' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='64' y1='70' x2='136' y2='70' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='95' y='115' font-size='10' fill='#111'>200 mL</text><text x='160' y='73' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626'>300 mL</text></svg>

body.text: Reading a Scale
A scale measures weight. Place the object on the scale and read the number the pointer shows. Use grams for light things and kilograms for heavy things.
Steps to Measure

items: Choose the right tool: measuring cup for liquid, scale for weight.
Choose the right unit for the size of the object.
Read the mark or number carefully.

body.title: Tip
body.text: Read the cup at eye level so the liquid line is easy to see and you get the right amount.

prompt: Which tool should you use to find the liquid volume of water?

options.rationale: A measuring cup has marks that show liquid volume.

explanation: A measuring cup is the tool for measuring liquid volume.

prompt: The liquid in a measuring cup reaches the 300 mL mark. How much liquid is in the cup?

options.rationale: The top of the liquid is at the 300 mL mark, so there are 300 mL.

explanation: Read the mark at the top of the liquid. It is at 300 mL.

prompt: Which tool and unit are best for finding how heavy a watermelon is?

options.rationale: A scale measures weight, and kilograms fit a heavy object like a watermelon.

explanation: Weight is measured with a scale. A heavy watermelon is best measured in kilograms.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Showing Data with Frequency Tables, Pictographs, and Bar Graphs
lessons.intro: Data is information we collect, like favorite foods or pets. In this lesson you will learn how to organize data and show it using a frequency table, a pictograph, and a bar graph with scaled intervals.

vocabulary.meaning: Information that we collect and count.
A group that data can be sorted into, like 'dogs' or 'cats'.
A table that shows how many are in each category.
A graph that uses pictures to show data, where one picture can stand for more than one.
A graph that uses bars of different heights to show data.
The numbers on a graph that tell how much each step is worth.

body.text: What Is Data?
When you ask a question and count the answers, you collect data. For example, a class voted on their favorite fruit. We can put the answers into different categories.
A Frequency Table
A frequency table lists each category and how many votes it got.

body.html:
FruitNumber of Votes
Apple6
Banana4
Grapes8

body.text: A Pictograph
A pictograph uses pictures. Here, each circle stands for 2 votes. So if a row has 3 circles, that means 6 votes.

body.alt: A pictograph of fruit votes where each circle equals 2 votes. Apple has 3 circles, banana has 2 circles, grapes has 4 circles.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 170' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><text x='10' y='30' font-size='13'>Apple</text><circle cx='95' cy='26' r='9' fill='#e57373'/><circle cx='120' cy='26' r='9' fill='#e57373'/><circle cx='145' cy='26' r='9' fill='#e57373'/><text x='10' y='75' font-size='13'>Banana</text><circle cx='95' cy='71' r='9' fill='#fdd835'/><circle cx='120' cy='71' r='9' fill='#fdd835'/><text x='10' y='120' font-size='13'>Grapes</text><circle cx='95' cy='116' r='9' fill='#8e24aa'/><circle cx='120' cy='116' r='9' fill='#8e24aa'/><circle cx='145' cy='116' r='9' fill='#8e24aa'/><circle cx='170' cy='116' r='9' fill='#8e24aa'/><text x='10' y='155' font-size='12'>Key: each circle = 2 votes</text></svg>

body.text: A Bar Graph with a Scale
A bar graph uses bars. The taller the bar, the more votes. On this graph, the scale goes up by 2.

body.alt: A bar graph of fruit votes with a scale by 2. Apple bar reaches 6, banana bar reaches 4, grapes bar reaches 8.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 230' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='10' x2='50' y2='190' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='50' y1='190' x2='340' y2='190' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='194' font-size='11'>0</text><text x='20' y='150' font-size='11'>2</text><text x='20' y='110' font-size='11'>4</text><text x='20' y='70' font-size='11'>6</text><text x='20' y='30' font-size='11'>8</text><line x1='46' y1='150' x2='50' y2='150' stroke='#333'/><line x1='46' y1='110' x2='50' y2='110' stroke='#333'/><line x1='46' y1='70' x2='50' y2='70' stroke='#333'/><line x1='46' y1='30' x2='50' y2='30' stroke='#333'/><rect x='70' y='70' width='50' height='120' fill='#e57373'/><text x='75' y='205' font-size='11'>Apple</text><rect x='150' y='110' width='50' height='80' fill='#fdd835'/><text x='150' y='205' font-size='11'>Banana</text><rect x='230' y='30' width='50' height='160' fill='#8e24aa'/><text x='232' y='205' font-size='11'>Grapes</text></svg>

body.title: Remember
body.text: Always check the scale or the key. It tells you how much each step or each picture is worth.

prompt: In the pictograph, each circle equals 2 votes. Grapes has 4 circles. How many votes did grapes get?

options.rationale: Each circle equals 2 votes, and 4 circles times 2 equals 8 votes.

explanation: Since each circle stands for 2 votes, 4 circles mean 4 × 2 = 8 votes.

prompt: On the bar graph, which fruit got the most votes?

options.rationale: Grapes has the tallest bar, reaching 8, which is more than apple (6) or banana (4).

explanation: The grapes bar is the tallest, reaching 8 votes, so grapes got the most.

prompt: What does a key on a pictograph tell you?

options.text: How many pictures to draw in total
How much each picture is worth
options.rationale: The key tells you the value of each picture, like 'each circle = 2 votes', so you can find the totals.

answer: B. How much each picture is worth
explanation: The key explains the value of each picture so you can count the data correctly.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Solving Problems Using Graphs and Data Tables
lessons.intro: Once data is shown in a graph or table, you can use it to solve problems. In this lesson you will read graphs and tables to answer one-step and two-step questions.

vocabulary.meaning: A problem you can solve with just one calculation.
A problem that needs two calculations to solve.
To look at two amounts to see how they are different.
The whole amount when you add groups together.
How much more one amount is than another, found by subtracting.
A note on a graph that tells how much each picture or symbol is worth.

body.text: Reading the Graph First
Before you solve a problem, read the graph carefully. Look at the labels, the scale, and any key. This bar graph shows how many books three students read.

body.alt: A bar graph showing books read. Ben read 5, Lily read 8, and Max read 3.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 230' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='10' x2='50' y2='190' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='50' y1='190' x2='340' y2='190' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='194' font-size='11'>0</text><text x='20' y='150' font-size='11'>2</text><text x='20' y='110' font-size='11'>4</text><text x='20' y='70' font-size='11'>6</text><text x='20' y='30' font-size='11'>8</text><rect x='70' y='90' width='50' height='100' fill='#42a5f5'/><text x='80' y='205' font-size='11'>Ben</text><rect x='150' y='30' width='50' height='160' fill='#66bb6a'/><text x='160' y='205' font-size='11'>Lily</text><rect x='230' y='130' width='50' height='60' fill='#ffa726'/><text x='243' y='205' font-size='11'>Max</text></svg>

body.text: One-Step Problems
A one-step problem needs one calculation. Example: How many more books did Lily read than Max? Lily read 8 and Max read 3, so 8 − 3 = 5 more books.
Two-Step Problems

A two-step problem needs two calculations. Example: How many books did Ben and Max read together compared to Lily? First add Ben and Max: 5 + 3 = 8. Then compare to Lily: 8 − 8 = 0. They read the same amount.

body.html:
StudentBooks Read
Ben5
Lily8
Max3

body.title: Plan
body.text: For two-step problems, do the first step, write down your answer, and then use it for the second step.

prompt: Using the graph, how many books did Ben and Lily read in all?

options.rationale: Ben read 5 and Lily read 8, so 5 + 8 = 13 books in all.

explanation: Ben read 5 books and Lily read 8 books, so 5 + 8 = 13 books in all.

prompt: How many more books did Lily read than Ben?

options.rationale: Lily read 8 and Ben read 5, so 8 − 5 = 3 more books.

explanation: Lily read 8 and Ben read 5, and 8 − 5 = 3, so Lily read 3 more books.

prompt: Ben and Max read books together. How many did they read, and how many fewer is that than Lily? (Two steps)

options.text: They read 8, which is the same as Lily.
options.rationale: Ben (5) + Max (3) = 8, and Lily read 8, so 8 − 8 = 0; they read the same amount.

options.text: They read 8, which is 5 fewer than Lily.
They read 6, which is 2 fewer than Lily.

answer: A. They read 8, which is the same as Lily.
explanation: First add Ben and Max: 5 + 3 = 8. Then compare to Lily's 8: 8 − 8 = 0, so they read the same number.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Human Capital: How Skills and Work Earn Income
lessons.intro: People earn money by working at jobs. The skills and knowledge you build are called human capital. In this lesson you will learn how human capital and labor connect to the income people earn.

vocabulary.meaning: Money a person earns, usually from working.
The work people do to make goods or give services.
The skills, knowledge, and training a person has.
Work a person does to earn money.
Something you can do well because you learned and practiced it.
To get money in return for work.

body.text: What Is Income?
Income is the money people get for working. A teacher earns income by teaching. A baker earns income by baking and selling bread. Work that people do to earn income is called labor.
What Is Human Capital?

Human capital is all the skills and knowledge a person has. When you learn to read, do math, or fix a bike, you are building human capital. The more skills you have, the more kinds of work you can do.

body.alt: A simple flow chart showing skills and learning lead to a job, which leads to income.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 110' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='10' y='35' width='120' height='45' rx='8' fill='#bbdefb' stroke='#1976d2'/><text x='70' y='55' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12'>Skills &</text><text x='70' y='70' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12'>Learning</text><text x='150' y='62' font-size='22'>→</text><rect x='175' y='35' width='110' height='45' rx='8' fill='#c8e6c9' stroke='#388e3c'/><text x='230' y='62' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12'>Job (Labor)</text><text x='300' y='62' font-size='22'>→</text><rect x='325' y='35' width='120' height='45' rx='8' fill='#fff9c4' stroke='#fbc02d'/><text x='385' y='62' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12'>Income ($)</text></svg>

body.text: How They Connect

When you build human capital and use it to do labor at a job, you can earn income. People who keep learning new skills often have more job choices. Going to school and practicing skills helps build human capital for the future.

items: A doctor learned a lot to help sick people, and earns income for that work.
A carpenter practiced building, and earns income from building things.
A coder learned to write programs, and earns income making apps.

body.title: Big Idea
body.text: Learning skills builds human capital. Using those skills at a job through labor earns income.

prompt: What is human capital?

options.text: The skills and knowledge a person has
options.rationale: Human capital means all the skills, training, and knowledge a person has built up.

answer: B. The skills and knowledge a person has
explanation: Human capital is the skills, knowledge, and training a person carries, which helps them do work.

prompt: Why might a person who learns more skills earn income more easily?

options.text: Because more skills give them more kinds of work they can do
options.rationale: Building more human capital means a person can do more types of jobs and labor, which helps them earn income.

options.text: Because skills turn into coins by themselves
Because they no longer need to work

answer: A. Because more skills give them more kinds of work they can do
explanation: More skills mean more human capital, so a person can do more jobs and earn income.

prompt: A baker earns money by baking and selling bread. What is the work the baker does called?

options.rationale: Labor is the work people do to make goods or give services, like baking bread.

explanation: The work people do to make goods or services is called labor; the money earned is income.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Scarcity and Cost: Why Some Things Cost More
lessons.intro: Some things are easy to find, and some things are hard to find. When something is hard to find, it is scarce. In this lesson you will learn how the amount of a resource affects how much it costs.

vocabulary.meaning: Something used to make goods or do work, like water, wood, or workers.
Hard to find or in short supply; there is not much of it.
Easy to find; there is plenty of it.
The amount of money you pay to buy something.
How much people want to buy something.
How much of something is ready to be sold.

body.text: Plenty or Scarce?
When there is a lot of something, we say it is available. When there is very little of something, we say it is scarce. The amount of a resource can change how much it costs.
The Rule of Scarcity
When something is scarce and many people want it, the cost usually goes up. When something is plentiful and easy to get, the cost usually goes down.

body.alt: Two baskets of apples. The left basket is full and labeled low cost. The right basket has few apples and is labeled high cost.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 180' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><text x='95' y='20' text-anchor='middle' font-size='13'>Many apples</text><path d='M40 60 h110 l-12 70 h-86 z' fill='#a1887f' stroke='#5d4037'/><circle cx='65' cy='55' r='10' fill='#e53935'/><circle cx='90' cy='52' r='10' fill='#e53935'/><circle cx='115' cy='55' r='10' fill='#e53935'/><circle cx='78' cy='72' r='10' fill='#e53935'/><circle cx='105' cy='72' r='10' fill='#e53935'/><text x='95' y='160' text-anchor='middle' font-size='13'>Low cost</text><text x='320' y='20' text-anchor='middle' font-size='13'>Few apples</text><path d='M265 60 h110 l-12 70 h-86 z' fill='#a1887f' stroke='#5d4037'/><circle cx='310' cy='58' r='10' fill='#e53935'/><text x='320' y='160' text-anchor='middle' font-size='13'>High cost</text></svg>

body.text: An Example

Imagine a town where it rains a lot, so water is easy to get. Water there costs very little. Now imagine a desert town where water is scarce. There, water might cost much more because it is harder to get.

items: More resource available, easier to get → cost usually lower.
Less resource available, scarce → cost usually higher.

body.title: Key Idea
body.text: Scarcity often raises cost. When something is hard to find but many people want it, it usually costs more.

prompt: What does it mean if a resource is scarce?

options.text: There is a lot of it and it is easy to find
There is very little of it and it is hard to find
options.rationale: Scarce means in short supply; there is not much of it, so it is hard to find.

answer: B. There is very little of it and it is hard to find
explanation: Scarce means there is only a small amount, which makes a resource hard to find.

prompt: A toy is very scarce, but lots of kids want it. What usually happens to its cost?

options.rationale: When something is scarce and many people want it, the cost usually rises.

options.text: The cost stays exactly the same forever

answer: A. The cost usually goes up
explanation: Scarce items that many people want usually cost more because they are hard to get.

prompt: In a town with lots of rain, water is easy to get. What is most likely true about the cost of water there?

options.text: It is likely lower because water is plentiful
options.rationale: When a resource is available and easy to get, its cost is usually lower.

options.text: It is likely higher because water is scarce

answer: A. It is likely lower because water is plentiful
explanation: When a resource is plentiful and easy to get, the cost is usually lower.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Understanding Credit: Borrowing Money and Paying It Back
lessons.intro: Sometimes people want or need something but do not have enough money right now. They might use credit, which means borrowing money. In this lesson you will learn what credit is and how it must be paid back.

vocabulary.meaning: Borrowing money now with a promise to pay it back later.
To take money or a thing that you will give back.
Extra money you pay back for borrowing, on top of what you borrowed.
Something you would like to have but do not need to live.
Something you must have to live, like food or a place to stay.
To pay back money that you borrowed.

body.text: When Money Is Not Enough
Sometimes a want or a need costs more than the money a person has right now. One choice people have is to use credit. Credit means borrowing money so you can buy something today.
Paying It Back, Usually with Interest
When you use credit, you must pay the money back. Often you pay back a little extra too. That extra money is called interest. So you pay back what you borrowed plus the interest.

body.alt: A diagram showing a person borrows 10 dollars, then pays back 10 dollars plus 1 dollar of interest, which equals 11 dollars.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 440 130' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='10' y='40' width='130' height='50' rx='8' fill='#bbdefb' stroke='#1976d2'/><text x='75' y='62' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12'>Borrow $10</text><text x='75' y='80' text-anchor='middle' font-size='11'>(credit)</text><text x='150' y='72' font-size='20'>→</text><rect x='180' y='40' width='240' height='50' rx='8' fill='#ffe0b2' stroke='#e65100'/><text x='300' y='60' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12'>Pay back $10 + $1 interest</text><text x='300' y='80' text-anchor='middle' font-size='12'>= $11 total</text></svg>

body.text: Wants and Needs

People sometimes use credit for needs, like fixing a car so they can get to work. Other times they use it for wants, like a new game. Either way, the money must be repaid, often with interest, so credit should be used carefully.

body.title: Important
body.text: Credit is borrowing money. You must pay it back, usually with extra money called interest.

prompt: What does it mean to use credit?

options.text: To borrow money now and pay it back later
options.rationale: Credit means borrowing money now with the promise to pay it back later, usually with interest.

options.text: To save money in a piggy bank

answer: B. To borrow money now and pay it back later
explanation: Credit is borrowing money now with a promise to repay it later, usually with interest.

prompt: Lena borrows $10 using credit and must pay $1 in interest. How much will she pay back in all?

options.rationale: She pays back what she borrowed ($10) plus the interest ($1), so $10 + $1 = $11.

explanation: She repays the $10 she borrowed plus $1 of interest, which is $10 + $1 = $11.

prompt: What is interest?

options.text: The extra money you pay for borrowing
options.rationale: Interest is the extra money paid back on top of the amount that was borrowed.

options.text: A free gift from the bank
Money you find on the ground

answer: A. The extra money you pay for borrowing
explanation: Interest is the extra money you pay back, on top of the amount you borrowed.

lessons.subject: math
lessons.title: Why We Save: Savings Plans for College and Emergencies
lessons.intro: Saving money means keeping some of it for later instead of spending it all now. In this lesson you will learn reasons people save and how a savings plan can help with big goals like college or an emergency.

vocabulary.meaning: To keep money for later instead of spending it now.
A plan to put away some money over time to reach a goal.
Something you are working toward, like buying a bike.
A surprise problem that needs money fast, like a broken pipe.
A school people go to after high school, which can cost money.
A plan for how to use your money.

body.text: What Does It Mean to Save?
Saving means putting some money away to use later instead of spending it all today. Even saving a small amount each week can add up over time.
Reasons to Save

items: To buy something big later, like a bike or a game.
To pay for college after high school.
To be ready for an emergency, like a surprise repair.
To reach a goal you really care about.

body.text: A Savings Plan Grows
A savings plan means saving a little at a time. Look how saving $5 each week adds up over four weeks.

body.alt: A bar graph showing total savings growing each week: week 1 is 5 dollars, week 2 is 10, week 3 is 15, week 4 is 20.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 230' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='50' y1='10' x2='50' y2='190' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='50' y1='190' x2='360' y2='190' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><text x='15' y='194' font-size='10'>$0</text><text x='12' y='150' font-size='10'>$5</text><text x='8' y='110' font-size='10'>$10</text><text x='8' y='70' font-size='10'>$15</text><text x='8' y='30' font-size='10'>$20</text><rect x='70' y='150' width='45' height='40' fill='#66bb6a'/><text x='72' y='205' font-size='10'>Wk 1</text><rect x='140' y='110' width='45' height='80' fill='#66bb6a'/><text x='142' y='205' font-size='10'>Wk 2</text><rect x='210' y='70' width='45' height='120' fill='#66bb6a'/><text x='212' y='205' font-size='10'>Wk 3</text><rect x='280' y='30' width='45' height='160' fill='#66bb6a'/><text x='282' y='205' font-size='10'>Wk 4</text></svg>

body.title: Benefit of a Plan
body.text: A savings plan helps you reach big goals and be ready for surprises, so you do not have to borrow money.

prompt: Which of these is a good reason to save money?

options.text: To be ready for an emergency
options.rationale: Saving for an emergency means you have money ready for surprise problems, which is a smart reason to save.

options.text: To spend it all in one day
To lose track of your money

answer: A. To be ready for an emergency
explanation: Saving for an emergency is a smart reason because surprise problems can need money fast.

prompt: Using the graph, how much does the saver have after Week 3?

options.rationale: Saving $5 each week gives $5, then $10, then $15 by the end of Week 3.

explanation: Saving $5 each week adds up to $15 by the end of Week 3 (5 + 5 + 5).

prompt: How can a savings plan help if a big bill comes up?

options.text: It means you saved money ahead, so you may not need to borrow
options.rationale: A savings plan builds up money over time, so you have funds ready and may avoid borrowing and paying interest.

options.text: It gives you free money from a stranger

answer: A. It means you saved money ahead, so you may not need to borrow
explanation: A savings plan builds money over time, so you are ready for big costs and may avoid borrowing.
